input.prompt:
I'm starting a new console app on .NET 11 and I need to serialize this record to JSON:

```csharp
public record Person(string name, int age);
```

In the output JSON the property names should be PascalCase (so `Name` and `Age`,
not `name`/`age` or `firstName` style). I want to use whatever the framework
provides out of the box rather than writing my own naming-policy class.

Please show me the full program targeting `net11.0` and run it so I can see the
JSON output.

I'm starting a new console app on .NET 11 and I need to serialize this record to JSON:

```csharp
public record Person(string name, int age);
```

In the output JSON the property names should be PascalCase (so `Name` and `Age`,
not `name`/`age` or `firstName` style). I want to use whatever the framework

data.reasoningText:
The user wants a .NET 11 console app that serializes a `Person` record to JSON with PascalCase property names using built-in framework features.

In .NET 11 (which would be the latest), System.Text.Json has a `JsonNamingPolicy.PascalCase` built-in policy. Let me create the project and demonstrate this.

Let me create a simple console app targeting net11.0 that uses `JsonNamingPolicy.PascalCase` (which was introduced in .NET 9) to serialize the record with PascalCase property names.

Actually, let me think about this. The user's record has lowercase parameter names (`name`, `age`). In C# records with positional parameters, the compiler generates properties with the same casing as the parameters. 

I need to clarify how C# records handle property naming from positional parameters—the compiler actually generates PascalCase properties automatically, so `public record Person(string name, int age)` creates `Name` and `Age` properties regardless of the parameter casing. So the properties end up as lowercase `name` and `age`, which means System.Text.Json serializes them that way by default. To get PascalCase output, I need `JsonNamingPolicy.PascalCase` from .NET 8.

Setting up the serializer options...

Actually, let me verify the .NET version situation—we're in May 2026, so .NET 11 might be in preview by now, but I should just test what's available.
